DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-27-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Atty. KIMBERLY LEVY, ESQ. of Dolan Law Firm (Tel: 415-421-2800) appearing for plaintiff Andrew Ruiter;
Atty. KARYN N. CHANDLER, ESQ. of Lewis Brisbois (Tel: 415-438-6639) appearing for defendany Lyft, Inc.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT LYFT, INC.'s DEMURRER TO PLAINTIFF'S FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Lyft, Inc.'s demurrer to Plaintiff Andrew Ruiter's first amended complaint is overruled. Joinder is proper here because there is reasonable uncertainty as to the extent of each Defendant's liability for Plaintiff's injuries. (See Code Civ. Proc. 379(c) ["Where the plaintiff is in doubt as to the person from whom he or she is entitled to redress, he or she may join two or more defendants, with the intent that the question as to which, if any, of the defendants is liable, and to what extent, may be determined between the parties."].) The first amended complaint pleads with sufficient particularity the injury, the negligence by successive tortfeasors, and doubt as to the extent of liability of each of the Defendants for his injuries. (See Landau v. Salam (1971) 4 Cal.3d 901, 905-908 [reversing trial court's order sustaining demurrer without leave to amend where complaint properly pled doubt as to liability for plaintiff's injuries and properly joined defendants as successive tortfeasors in separate motor vehicle collision and premises liability incidents that occurred over three months apart].) Specifically, the first amended complaint pleads injuries to Plaintiff's head, neck, and upper extremities from the first accident-involving Lyft driver Defendant Leonard Rodrigues De Souza-and that the second accident-involving Defendants Sing Chim Shiu and Mai Xue Fang-caused new injuries and may have aggravated the injuries to Plaintiff's neck and upper extremities. (FAC 15, 19-20, 23-25.) Lyft's objection that joinder will cause it additional expense and burden is misplaced. (See Landau v. Salam, 4 Cal.3d at 908 ["We recognize that if we permit a single trial of the two causes, some parties will encounter some inconvenience. But the minor travail must succumb to the larger loss; the bifurcation of the litigation would entail the greater expenditure of time and energy."].) The cases relied upon by Lyft are distinguishable. Jensen v. Home Depot, Inc. (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 92, 94 did not involve intertwined injuries and the need to allocate fault and liability, but rather the misjoinder of two different plaintiffs whose claims did not arise out of the same transaction or raise common issues of law or fact. Likewise, Hoag v. Superior Court (1962) 207 Cal.App.2d 611, 617-620 involved four entirely different transactions and the court concluded that the plaintiff in that case was seeking joinder for the ulterior motive of forum shopping.

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano, CSR#8600, e-mail: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)
